food_subgroup: Fried poultry — spiced and breaded chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken food product class; no Cajun-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Cajun fried chicken entry. A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Cajun fried chicken or fried chicken dish found in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

main_ingredients: Cajun spice blend (paprika, cayenne pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, thyme, black pepper)

serving.description: 1 half chicken portion (approximately 350 g edible portion with bone-in weight ~500 g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is half a Cajun fried chicken, yielding approximately 350 g of edible meat and skin, commonly served with fries.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 05062)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for batter-fried chicken (meat and skin) used as the closest approved analogue. Cajun seasoning adds negligible macronutrient change; sodium adjusted upward by approximately 80 mg/100 g to reflect Cajun spice blend salt contribution, consistent with USDA Branded Food data for Cajun-seasoned fried chicken products. Values represent the edible portion including skin.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cajun fried chicken. GI estimated at 45 based on batter-fried chicken analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Fried chicken with wheat-flour batter has a moderate-low GI due to the protein and fat matrix slowing glucose absorption. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 8.2 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 12.9, rounded to 16.4 using full serving carbohydrate (8.2 g/100 g × 350 g serving = 28.7 g carbohydrate; GL = 45 × 28.7 / 100 = 12.9). Value reported as 12.9 in notes; field set to 12.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~490 mg/100 g; ~1715 mg per half-chicken serving) from Cajun spice blend and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Cajun fried chicken to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt seasoning where possible, as a single half-chicken serving may provide approximately 74% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from wheat-flour batter combined with high fat content, which may contribute to postprandial glycaemic excursion and caloric excess
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Cajun fried chicken in controlled portions, preferring skinless pieces to reduce fat intake, and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than fries to moderate the overall glycaemic and caloric load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.4 g/100 g), high sodium, and deep-frying in vegetable oil contributing to total fat and potential trans fat exposure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Cajun fried chicken as an occasional food, limiting portion size to minimise saturated fat and sodium intake, and should avoid regular consumption as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern consistent with AHA guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cajun Fried Chicken is a deep-fried chicken dish originating from the Cajun culinary tradition of Louisiana, United States. Chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs, drumsticks, or half-bird portions — are marinated in buttermilk, coated in seasoned wheat flour containing a Cajun spice blend of paprika, cayenne pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, dried oregano, thyme, and black pepper, then deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is deeply golden and crisp while the interior remains juicy. The dish is characterised by its bold, smoky, and moderately spicy flavour profile with a crunchy crust and tender, moist meat. In Singapore, it is served at casual dining and fast-casual restaurants, often accompanied by French fries or coleslaw. Nutritionally, Cajun fried chicken is energy-dense (~277 kcal/100 g), high in protein (~23.5 g/100 g), and moderate-to-high in fat (~16.8 g/100 g), with a significant sodium content (~490 mg/100 g) from the spice rub. The glycaemic index is estimated at 45 (low), though the glycaemic load per half-chicken serving is meaningful (~12.9) due to the batter coating. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat content, and requires portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Cajun fried chicken?
Can diabetics eat Cajun fried chicken?
How much sodium is in Cajun fried chicken?
What spices are in Cajun fried chicken?
Cajun chicken vs regular fried chicken

confidence_reason: No exact Cajun fried chicken entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy batter-fried chicken (NDB 05062) with sodium adjustment for Cajun seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried chicken data. Confidence is medium due to the adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried chicken. Sodium value adjusted upward from USDA baseline (~410 mg/100 g) by approximately 80 mg/100 g to account for Cajun spice blend salt contribution, supported by USDA Branded Food entries for Cajun-seasoned fried chicken products. Vitamin C is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — fried chicken with batter contains no meaningful vitamin C. The food is tagged as Indian cuisine in the field survey source record, but Cajun fried chicken is a Louisiana/Southern American dish; cuisine corrected to American (Cajun/Southern) per culinary origin. The field survey listing at #Foodcoholic (40 Circular Road, Singapore) represents a single restaurant offering. Halal status set to true as the restaurant context is Singapore Indian-cuisine restaurant likely using halal chicken; this should be verified against restaurant certification. GL per serving recalculated: 45 × (8.2 g/100 g × 350 g) / 100 = 45 × 28.7 / 100 = 12.9. glycemic_load_per_serving field set to 12.9.
